weeks.title: Week 1 — Spring Boot setup + first REST endpoints
weeks.description: Get your first Spring Boot app running and understand how the framework is structured.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Create your first Spring Boot project

tasks.text: Go to start.spring.io — choose Maven, Java 21, Spring Boot 3.x (latest stable)
Add dependencies: Spring Web, Spring Data JPA, MySQL Driver, Lombok
Open the project in IntelliJ — explore the folder structure
Understand: what is @SpringBootApplication and the main() method
Configure application.properties: datasource URL, username, password, ddl-auto=update
Run the app — confirm it starts without errors in the console

days.resource: Note: Spring Boot 3.x uses Jakarta EE (not javax). When you see import errors, use jakarta.* not javax.*

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: First REST controller

tasks.text: Learn: @RestController vs @Controller — what's the difference
Learn: @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping, @DeleteMapping
Learn: @RequestMapping for base path on a controller class
Build: HelloController with GET /api/hello returning a String
Test: open browser or Postman, hit http://localhost:8080/api/hello
Install Postman and create a collection called "Product API"

days.resource: Resource: Dan Vega on YouTube — "Building REST APIs with Spring Boot" (free, 2024 version)

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Request handling — params, path vars, body

tasks.text: Learn: @PathVariable — GET /api/products/{id}
Learn: @RequestParam — GET /api/products?page=0&size=10
Learn: @RequestBody — receive JSON in POST/PUT requests
Learn: ResponseEntity — control HTTP status codes in your response
Build: ProductController with in-memory List (no DB yet) — GET all, GET by id, POST, DELETE

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Layered architecture — Service layer

tasks.text: Learn: why separate Controller, Service, Repository layers
Learn: @Service annotation and Dependency Injection via constructor
Learn: how Spring's IoC container manages beans automatically
Refactor: move business logic out of controller into ProductService
Inject ProductService into ProductController using constructor injection

days.resource: Rule to remember: Controller talks to Service. Service talks to Repository. Repository talks to DB. Never skip a layer.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: Exception handling

tasks.text: Learn: what happens when your controller throws an unhandled exception
Learn: @ControllerAdvice and @ExceptionHandler
Build: a custom ResourceNotFoundException class
Build: a GlobalExceptionHandler that returns JSON error responses
Test: request a product by a non-existent ID — confirm clean 404 JSON response

days.resource: Resource: Amigoscode "Spring Boot Exception Handling" on YouTube

weeks.askPrompt: Show me the exact folder structure and code for a Spring Boot 3 project with Controller, Service, and a GlobalExceptionHandler — beginner-friendly with comments

weeks.title: Week 2 — Spring Data JPA + MySQL
weeks.description: Replace your in-memory list with a real database. This is where it gets exciting.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: JPA entities and the database layer

tasks.text: Learn: what JPA is — a standard for mapping Java objects to DB tables
Learn: @Entity, @Table, @Id, @GeneratedValue(strategy=IDENTITY)
Learn: @Column — nullable, unique, length constraints
Learn: Lombok @Data, @NoArgsConstructor, @AllArgsConstructor to cut boilerplate
Build: Product entity (id, name, description, price, stock, createdAt)
Run app — verify MySQL Workbench shows the products table was auto-created

days.resource: Note: Use ddl-auto=update in dev only. Never in production — use Flyway (covered in Phase 5).

tasks.text: Learn: JpaRepository<Entity, IdType> — what methods you get for free
Build: ProductRepository interface that extends JpaRepository
Update ProductService to use ProductRepository (inject it via constructor)
Test: POST a product in Postman — confirm it saves to MySQL
Test: GET /api/products — confirm it returns from DB
Test: DELETE by id — confirm row removed in MySQL Workbench

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Custom queries + pagination

tasks.text: Learn: derived query methods — findByName(), findByPriceLessThan()
Learn: @Query annotation with JPQL for custom queries
Learn: Pageable and Page<T> for paginated results
Build: GET /api/products?page=0&size=5&sort=price,asc — paginated endpoint
Build: GET /api/products/search?name=laptop — search by name

days.resource: Resource: Spring Data JPA docs — "Query Methods" section at docs.spring.io

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Entity relationships — @OneToMany

tasks.text: Learn: @ManyToOne and @OneToMany — a Category has many Products
Learn: @JoinColumn — specifies the foreign key column
Learn: FetchType.LAZY vs EAGER — always use LAZY, fetch explicitly when needed
Learn: cascade types — when saving parent also saves children
Build: Category entity with @OneToMany to Product
Test: create a category, assign products to it, query products by category

days.resource: Warning: Never use FetchType.EAGER on collections — it causes the N+1 problem and silently destroys performance.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: DTOs — separate your API from your entities

tasks.text: Learn: why you should never return JPA entities directly from controllers
Learn: DTO (Data Transfer Object) pattern — a plain class for API input/output
Build: ProductRequest DTO (for incoming POST/PUT body)
Build: ProductResponse DTO (for outgoing JSON — only expose safe fields)
Update service to map entity ↔ DTO manually (or use MapStruct)

days.resource: Why it matters: Returning raw entities leaks internal fields (like passwords), causes JSON infinite loops on bidirectional relations, and tightly couples your DB schema to your API.

weeks.askPrompt: Explain the JPA N+1 problem to me with a simple example and show me how to fix it using @EntityGraph or JOIN FETCH in Spring Boot

weeks.title: Week 3 — Validation + advanced JPA
weeks.description: Make your API bulletproof — reject bad data before it touches the database.

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-validation
Learn: @NotNull, @NotBlank, @Size, @Min, @Max, @Email on DTO fields
Learn: add @Valid to @RequestBody parameter in controller
Learn: handle MethodArgumentNotValidException in GlobalExceptionHandler
Test: POST a product with a blank name — confirm 400 with helpful error message
Build: return a map of field → error message in validation failure response
Learn: @ManyToMany — a Product can have many Tags, a Tag can have many Products
Learn: @JoinTable — defines the join table (product_tags) with both foreign keys
Learn: owning side vs inverse side of the relationship
Build: Tag entity + @ManyToMany on Product
Test: create tags, assign to products, query products by tag
Learn: @EntityListeners(AuditingEntityListener.class)
Learn: @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate on timestamp fields
Learn: @EnableJpaAuditing on your main class or a config class
Build: an abstract BaseEntity class with audit fields — all entities extend it
Test: create a product, confirm createdAt and updatedAt auto-populate

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Service layer patterns

tasks.text: Learn: @Transactional — when and why to add it to service methods
Learn: readOnly=true for GET service methods — performance hint to JPA
Learn: how to do a partial update (PATCH) — only update non-null fields
Build: PATCH /api/products/{id} endpoint with partial update logic
Test: PATCH with only price changed — confirm name untouched in DB
Learn: @EnableCaching, @Cacheable("products") on getAllProducts() — add @CacheEvict on create/update/delete so the cache stays fresh

days.resource: Pattern: For PATCH, fetch entity from DB, check each DTO field for null, update only non-null fields, save. Never blindly overwrite.

tasks.text: Learn: RESTful URL conventions — nouns not verbs (/products not /getProducts)
Learn: correct HTTP status codes — 200, 201, 204, 400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 500
Learn: consistent error response format — include status, message, timestamp, path
Learn: API versioning with URL prefix — /api/v1/products
Refactor: update all your endpoints to follow these conventions

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to implement @Transactional correctly in a Spring Boot service layer — with a real example of when it matters and what happens without it

weeks.title: Week 4 — Spring Security + JWT authentication
weeks.description: The hardest week. Take it slow — Security trips up every beginner. Understand each step before moving on.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Spring Security basics

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-security
Observe: all your endpoints are now locked behind HTTP Basic auth
Learn: the Security Filter Chain concept — requests go through filters before hitting controllers
Learn: @EnableWebSecurity and writing a SecurityFilterChain @Bean
Learn: how to permit certain URLs (e.g. /api/v1/auth/**) and require auth on others
Build: User entity with email, password, role fields

days.resource: Resource: Amigoscode "Spring Boot 3 + Spring Security 6 + JWT" on YouTube — watch Day 1 portion only today

tasks.text: Learn: UserDetails interface — what Spring Security expects from a user object
Make your User entity implement UserDetails (or wrap it in a separate class)
Learn: UserDetailsService — loadUserByUsername() method Spring calls during login
Build: UserDetailsServiceImpl that loads user from DB by email
Learn: BCryptPasswordEncoder — never store plain text passwords
Register PasswordEncoder @Bean — hash password on registration, verify on login

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: JWT token generation

tasks.text: Add dependency: io.jsonwebtoken:jjwt-api, jjwt-impl, jjwt-jackson
Learn: JWT structure — header.payload.signature
Learn: what goes in the claims — subject (email), issued at, expiration
Build: JwtService with generateToken() and extractUsername() methods
Store JWT secret and expiration in application.properties (externalized)
Test: generate a token, paste into jwt.io to inspect the claims

days.resource: Resource: jwt.io — paste any JWT token here to decode and inspect it visually

tasks.text: Build: JwtAuthenticationFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter
In filter: read Authorization header, extract Bearer token, validate, set SecurityContext
Register the filter before UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter in SecurityFilterChain
Build: AuthController with POST /api/v1/auth/register and /login
Build: AuthService — register saves hashed password, login verifies and returns JWT
Learn: roles — USER vs ADMIN — store as enum in User entity
Learn: .hasRole() and .hasAuthority() in SecurityFilterChain
Learn: @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") on controller methods
Configure CORS in SecurityFilterChain to allow your frontend origin
Test full flow in Postman: register → login → get token → use token on protected endpoint
Build: GET /api/v1/auth/me — use @AuthenticationPrincipal UserDetails to return the current user as a UserResponse DTO (never the password)

weeks.askPrompt: Walk me through building a complete JWT authentication system in Spring Boot 3 step by step — from User entity to JwtAuthenticationFilter — with code for each class

weeks.title: Week 5 — File uploads + email + testing
weeks.description: Round out your API with real-world features and make sure it works correctly.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Multipart file upload

tasks.text: Learn: @RequestParam MultipartFile file in controller
Learn: file.getOriginalFilename(), file.getBytes(), file.getInputStream()
Configure: spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size in application.properties
Build: POST /api/v1/products/{id}/image — saves file to local /uploads/ folder
Build: GET endpoint to serve the uploaded image back
Test: upload a product image via Postman form-data

days.resource: Next step: In Phase 4 you'll swap local storage for Cloudflare R2 or AWS S3 — same API, different storage backend.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Sending emails with JavaMailSender

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-mail
Configure: SMTP settings in application.properties (use Mailtrap.io for testing)
Build: EmailService with sendSimpleEmail() method using JavaMailSender
Trigger: send welcome email on user registration
Test: register a user, check Mailtrap inbox for the email

days.resource: Resource: mailtrap.io — free fake SMTP inbox for dev/testing. No real emails sent.

tasks.text: Learn: what a unit test is — test one class in isolation
Learn: @Test, @BeforeEach, Assertions.assertEquals(), assertThrows()
Learn: Mockito — @Mock, @InjectMocks, when().thenReturn(), verify()
Write: unit tests for ProductService — mock the repository, test business logic
Write: test the "product not found" exception is thrown correctly
Run tests — aim for green on all service tests
Learn: @WebMvcTest — loads only web layer, mocks everything else
Learn: MockMvc — perform HTTP requests in tests without a running server
Write: test GET /api/v1/products returns 200 with correct JSON body
Write: test POST with invalid body returns 400 with validation errors
Write: test authenticated endpoint returns 401 without JWT
Add H2 (test scope) + application-test.properties (ddl-auto=create-drop) — write a @SpringBootTest integration test for the full register → login → use-token flow

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: Logging + environment config

tasks.text: Learn: SLF4J with Logback — @Slf4j (Lombok) and log.info(), log.error(), log.debug()
Add meaningful log statements to service methods (not System.out.println!)
Learn: Spring profiles — application-dev.properties vs application-prod.properties
Move DB credentials and JWT secret to environment variables
Never commit secrets to Git — add application-prod.properties to .gitignore

days.resource: Rule: If it's a secret (password, API key, JWT secret), it lives in an environment variable — never in code or committed properties files.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to write a JUnit 5 unit test for a Spring Boot service class using Mockito — with @Mock, @InjectMocks, and testing an exception scenario

weeks.title: Week 6 — Polish, Postman docs + deployment prep
weeks.description: Make your API production-ready and deploy it so the world can see it.

tasks.text: Add dependency: springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui
Visit http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html — your API is auto-documented
Learn: @Operation, @Parameter, @ApiResponse annotations for richer docs
Configure Swagger to support JWT — add Authorize button for Bearer token
Test: use Swagger UI to call your endpoints — confirm auth works through UI

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Deploy to Railway

tasks.text: Create Railway account at railway.app
Add a MySQL service to your Railway project
Connect your GitHub repo to Railway — enable auto-deploy on push to main
Set environment variables in Railway dashboard (DB_URL, DB_USER, DB_PASS, JWT_SECRET)
Deploy — watch the Railway build logs, fix any startup errors
Test your live API URL in Postman — full register/login/CRUD flow

days.resource: Tip: Railway gives you a public URL like yourapp.up.railway.app — save this, you'll connect your React frontend to it in Phase 4.

tasks.text: Organise all your requests into folders in Postman (Auth, Products, Categories)
Create a Postman environment with {{baseUrl}} and {{token}} variables
Add a Postman test script on the login request to auto-save token to environment
Add request examples (sample bodies) to every endpoint in the collection
Export collection and commit it to GitHub in a /postman folder
Review each class — does every method do exactly one thing?
Remove all System.out.println() — replace with proper log statements
Check all endpoints return the right HTTP status code (POST returns 201, DELETE returns 204)
Ensure no entity is ever returned directly — only DTOs
Write a README.md with project setup steps, env vars needed, and API endpoint list
Run your full Postman collection against the live Railway URL — everything green?
Note your live API base URL — you'll need it in Phase 3
Install Node.js and npm — required to start Phase 3 (React)
Scaffold a Vite React project: npm create vite@latest — just to preview what's coming
Push everything to GitHub — backend repo should be clean and well-documented

Self-assessment: without notes, draw the layered architecture (Controller → Service → Repository → DB), explain how JWT auth flows through the filter chain, and write a JPA entity with a @ManyToOne relationship from memory

weeks.askPrompt: How do I set up Springdoc OpenAPI Swagger UI in Spring Boot 3 with JWT authorization support — show me the config and dependency

weeks.title: Phase 2 project — Product Catalog REST API
weeks.description: Your complete backend, tested in Postman and deployed live on Railway.

projectCards.heading: What you'll build
projectCards.text: A production-ready REST API for an e-commerce product catalog with user registration, JWT login, role-based access, full product and category CRUD, image uploads, search, pagination, and live deployment.

projectCards.heading: Entities
projectCards.text: User (id, name, email, password, role) · Category (id, name, slug) · Product (id, name, description, price, stock, imageUrl, category, createdAt, updatedAt) · Tag (id, name) · Product_Tags join table

tasks.text: POST /api/v1/auth/register — create user account
POST /api/v1/auth/login — returns JWT token
GET /api/v1/auth/me — returns current user from token
GET /api/v1/products — paginated, public
GET /api/v1/products/{id} — public
GET /api/v1/products/search?name= — public
POST /api/v1/products — ADMIN only
PUT /api/v1/products/{id} — ADMIN only
DELETE /api/v1/products/{id} — ADMIN only
POST /api/v1/products/{id}/image — upload product image
CRUD for /api/v1/categories
Swagger UI live at /swagger-ui.html
Deployed and accessible on Railway public URL
GET /api/v1/products cached with @Cacheable, evicted on create/update/delete
Integration test (H2 + @SpringBootTest) covering register → login → use-token flow

weeks.tip: A live, documented REST API with JWT auth is a strong portfolio piece on its own. When this is done, you have the backend every React frontend in Phase 3 will connect to.
weeks.askPrompt: Help me start building the Phase 2 project — a Product Catalog REST API. Begin with the User entity, AuthController, and JWT setup in Spring Boot 3.
